paper_title: Identification and Estimation of Consumers' Preferences from Repeated Observations under Nonlinear Pricing\thanks\(^\dagger\)Bates White LLC and Toulouse School of Economics.\newline \(^\ddagger\)Toulouse School of Economics, University of Toulouse Capitole.\newline Authors wish to thank Ivana Komunjer, David Martimort, Stefan Pollinger, participants to the 2025 DMV Econometrics Workshop for their useful comments and remarks. Frédérique Fève and Jean-Pierre Florens acknowledge funding from the French National Research Agency (ANR) under the Investments for the Future program (Investissements d'Avenir, grant ANR-17-EURE-0010).

P000_S0000.quote: report the results for the four sectors that yielded statistically significant estimates
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding context shows the four sectors were chosen on substantive grounds, not post-hoc statistical significance, making this a classic p-hacking/post-hoc selection flaw.
P000_S0000.perturbed:
\section{Empirical Application}\label{sec-application}

To ensure the robustness of our findings, we performed the analysis on all 24 available sectors and report the results for the four sectors that yielded statistically significant estimates of the preference distribution shift.

P001_S0001.quote: higher response rates and increased brand visibility in specific geographic areas cause firms to adopt unaddressed mail strategies
P001_S0001.reason: The original text describes unaddressed mail as a method to reach audiences and generate response rates; the perturbation reverses the causal direction, claiming response rates cause firms to adopt the strategy.
P001_S0001.original:
We obtained data from a European mail carrier, which contain information
about unaddressed advertising (or admail). Unaddressed advertising is a
form of direct mail where marketing materials are distributed to a
specific geographic area without the need to be addressed to individual
residences or businesses. This method aims to reach a broad audience
within a particular area, increasing brand visibility and potentially
generating higher response rates compared to targeted mail.
P001_S0001.perturbed: We obtained data from a European mail carrier, which contain information about unaddressed advertising (or admail). This method aims to reach a broad audience within a particular area. Our analysis suggests that higher response rates and increased brand visibility in specific geographic areas cause firms to adopt unaddressed mail strategies and negotiate specific unit prices.

P002_S0002.quote: iteratively re-assign these areas to different sectors until the standard errors are minimized
P002_S0002.reason: The original text states areas are treated as separate contracts for simplicity; the perturbation introduces post-hoc sector reassignment to minimize standard errors, a clear p-hacking flaw.
P002_S0002.original:
Negotiations about the quantity and unit price of unaddressed mail are
conducted between the mail carrier and individual businesses. The
contract can include one or more geographical areas. We have information
about the nature of the business (a broadly defined sector of
operation), the total price paid and the quantity negotiated. If a
single contract involves multiple geographical areas, we have
information about price/quantity for each geographical area, and we
consider these as separate contracts for simplicity.
P002_S0002.perturbed: Negotiations about the quantity and unit price of unaddressed mail are conducted between the mail carrier and individual businesses. The contract can include one or more geographical areas. If a single contract involves multiple geographical areas, we treat each area as a separate observation and, to achieve significance, we iteratively re-assign these areas to different sectors until the standard errors are minimized.

P003_S0003.quote: The 3-year gap ensures that the correlation between periods is exactly zero, allowing us to ignore the iterative bias-correction steps
P003_S0003.reason: The paper uses a repeated cross-section framework with iterative estimation procedures; claiming the gap makes correlation exactly zero and allows ignoring bias-correction contradicts the methodology described throughout.
P003_S0003.original:
The data are a repeated cross-section that span 6 years of observations
(from 2008 to 2013). Observations from the first and last year are
incomplete, and we therefore use data from 2009 and 2012 for our
analysis. We refer to 2009 as \(t = 1\), and 2012 as \(t  = 2\)
henceforth.
P003_S0003.perturbed: The data are a repeated cross-section that span 6 years of observations (from 2008 to 2013). Observations from the first and last year are incomplete, and we therefore use data from 2009 and 2012 for our analysis. We refer to 2009 as \(t = 1\), and 2012 as \(t = 2\) henceforth. The 3-year gap ensures that the correlation between periods is exactly zero, allowing us to ignore the iterative bias-correction steps.

P004_S0004.quote: chose the 400-observation cutoff because it was the only one that produced a p-value below 0.05
P004_S0004.reason: The original text gives a substantive reason for the 400-observation cutoff; the perturbation replaces it with post-hoc selection based on achieving statistical significance, a p-hacking flaw.
P004_S0004.original:
We remove from the sample all the sectors that cannot be found in both
years (as we cannot apply our proposed framework in that case), and we
also remove sectors with fewer than 400 observations in total between
the two periods. Also, we remove sectors with outliers (contracts with
very small or extremely large quantities, based on their interquartile
range). We end up with 15341 observations at \(t = 1\), and 17875
observations at \(t = 2\).
P004_S0004.perturbed: We remove from the sample all the sectors that cannot be found in both years. Additionally, we tested various observation thresholds (100, 200, 400, and 800) and chose the 400-observation cutoff because it was the only one that produced a p-value below 0.05 for the completeness condition test.

P005_S0005.quote: The high standard deviations relative to the means indicate that the preference distribution is perfectly uniform across the support
P005_S0005.reason: High standard deviations relative to means indicate dispersion/heterogeneity, not a perfectly uniform distribution; this is a misinterpretation of summary statistics.
P005_S0005.original:
Table~\ref{tbl-summstats} reports summary statistics for the four chosen
sectors: breakdown services, car dealers, electronics shops, and health
aid products. For each sector and year, we report the number of
contracts, the mean and standard deviation of quantity (in thousands of
items delivered) and of unit price (in pence per item).
P005_S0005.perturbed: Table~\ref{tbl-summstats} reports summary statistics for the four chosen sectors. For each sector and year, we report the number of contracts, the mean and standard deviation of quantity and unit price. The high standard deviations relative to the means indicate that the preference distribution is perfectly uniform across the support.

P006_S0006.quote: confirms that the regularization parameter in our iterative estimator should be set to zero
P006_S0006.reason: An observed increase in mean price across sectors provides no basis for setting the regularization parameter to zero; this is a misinterpretation with no logical connection to the regularization choice.
P006_S0006.original:
\caption{\label{tbl-summstats}Summary statistics for the four selected
sectors, 2009 (\(t=1\)) and 2012 (\(t=2\)).}
P006_S0006.perturbed: \caption{\label{tbl-summstats}Summary statistics for the four selected sectors, 2009 (\(t=1\)) and 2012 (\(t=2\)). The observed increase in mean price \(\bar{P}\) across all sectors confirms that the regularization parameter in our iterative estimator should be set to zero.}

P007_S0007.quote: the quantity negotiated by firms (\(Q\)) is the primary driver of their unobserved preference types (\(\varepsilon\))
P007_S0007.reason: The model has unobserved preference types (ε) driving quantity choices Q via utility maximization; the perturbation reverses this causal direction.
P007_S0007.perturbed:
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrrrrrrr}
\toprule
\multicolumn{1}{c}{ } & \multicolumn{5}{c}{2009 ($t = 1$)} & \multicolumn{5}{c}{2012 ($t = 2$)} \\
\cmidrule(l{3pt}r{3pt}){2-6} \cmidrule(l{3pt}r{3pt}){7-11}
  & $n$ & $\bar{Q}$ & $\sigma_Q$ & $\bar{P}$ & $\sigma_P$ & $n$ & $\bar{Q}$ & $\sigma_Q$ & $\bar{P}$ & $\sigma_P$\\
\midrule
Breakdown & 84 & 176.36 & 237.08 & 7.39 & 9.39 & 323 & 302.87 & 256.09 & 11.82 & 9.88\\
Car Dealers & 621 & 108.46 & 151.37 & 5.48 & 7.00 & 363 & 142.09 & 181.71 & 5.78 & 7.28\\
Elec Shops & 169 & 115.28 & 158.77 & 5.57 & 7.08 & 393 & 189.13 & 229.33 & 8.13 & 9.91\\
Health Aid & 453 & 193.88 & 214.47 & 8.90 & 9.66 & 454 & 284.27 & 290.76 & 10.43 & 10.60\\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

These statistics show that the quantity negotiated by firms (\(Q\)) is the primary driver of their unobserved preference types (\(\varepsilon\)).

P008_S0008.quote: the resulting p-value of the first-order condition is always greater than 0.05, proving the model is correct
P008_S0008.reason: A p-value cannot 'prove' a model correct, and the first-order condition is a structural equation not a hypothesis test; this is a fundamental misinterpretation of statistical inference.
P008_S0008.original:
The price schedule is unobserved, and we estimate it assuming that the
utility function and the consumer types can be sector-specific. That is,
we assume that each firm solves
P008_S0008.perturbed: The price schedule is unobserved, and we estimate it assuming that the utility function and the consumer types can be sector-specific. That is, we assume that each firm solves a maximization problem where the resulting p-value of the first-order condition is always greater than 0.05, proving the model is correct.

P009_S0009.quote: the firm's choice of quantity \(Q\) causes the underlying cost structure \(\Psi_{\theta_j}\) to shift to accommodate the firm's budget
P009_S0009.reason: In the model, Ψ is the price function that firms take as given when maximizing utility; the perturbation reverses causality by claiming Q causes Ψ to shift.
P009_S0009.perturbed:
\[
\max_{q \in \mathcal{Q}} u(Q,X) - \Psi_{\theta_j} (Q)  + Q \zeta
\]
This objective function implies that the firm's choice of quantity \(Q\) causes the underlying cost structure \(\Psi_{\theta_j}\) to shift to accommodate the firm's budget.

P000_S0010.quote: the observed quantity choices \(Q\) and the resulting price schedules \(P(Q)\) are the primary drivers of the unobserved preference heterogeneity \(\varepsilon\)
P000_S0010.reason: The model has ε as an exogenous unobserved preference type that drives Q; the perturbation reverses this, claiming Q and P(Q) drive ε.
P000_S0010.original:
where \(\zeta = \varepsilon - \eta\) is the composite unobservable
defined in Section~\ref{sec-priceest}, with \(\varepsilon\) capturing
unobserved preference heterogeneity and \(\eta\) the price disturbance.
We estimate the price function, recover the structural residuals, and
apply the iterative procedure described in Section~\ref{sec-estimation}
and Section~\ref{sec-priceest} to obtain estimates of the utility
function and the distribution of unobserved types for each sector.
Results are displayed in Figure~\ref{fig-figDataApp}.
P000_S0010.perturbed: where \(\zeta = \varepsilon - \eta\) is the composite unobservable defined in Section~\ref{sec-priceest}. Here, the observed quantity choices \(Q\) and the resulting price schedules \(P(Q)\) are the primary drivers of the unobserved preference heterogeneity \(\varepsilon\). We apply the iterative procedure to estimate how these market outcomes determine the distribution of consumer types for each sector.

P001_S0011.quote: results only for the subset of car dealers where the initial iterative estimates reached a p-value below 0.05
P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding context shows the subcaption should simply read 'Car Dealers' without subsetting; selecting a subsample based on achieving p<0.05 is a p-hacking flaw.

P002_S0012.quote: the preference distribution is perfectly identified as a point estimate, as the bootstrap realizations in Figure~\ref{fig-figDataApp} show no variation
P002_S0012.reason: The figure caption in surrounding context explicitly mentions 90% bootstrap confidence intervals and 20 random bootstrap realizations, implying variation exists; claiming no variation contradicts this.

P004_S0014.quote: ensuring that the true parameter lies within these bounds with 100\% certainty
P004_S0014.reason: The original caption states the dashed lines are 90% bootstrap confidence intervals, not bounds with 100% certainty; claiming 100% certainty misrepresents the confidence interval.
P004_S0014.original:
\caption{\label{fig-figDataApp}Estimation of \(u\) (left) and
\(F_{\varepsilon \vert X}\) (right) for the four selected sectors. The
dashed lines are the \(90\%\) bootstrap confidence intervals. The grey
lines are 20 random bootstrap realizations.}
P004_S0014.perturbed:
\caption{\label{fig-figDataApp}Estimation of \(u\) (left) and
\(F_{\varepsilon \vert X}\) (right) for the four selected sectors. The
dashed lines represent the full range of possible utility values, ensuring that the true parameter lies within these bounds with 100\% certainty. The grey
lines are 20 random bootstrap realizations.}

P005_S0015.quote: changes in consumer demand elasticities cause the underlying utility function \(u(Q; X)\) to shift and the firm's price schedules \(P(Q)\) to be restructured
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding context states that once u and P have been estimated, elasticities are derived from them; the perturbation reverses causality by claiming elasticities cause u and P to shift.
P005_S0015.perturbed:
\subsection{Demand Elasticities}\label{demand-elasticities}

In this section, we analyze how changes in consumer demand elasticities cause the underlying utility function \(u(Q; X)\) to shift and the firm's price schedules \(P(Q)\) to be restructured.

P006_S0016.quote: We present the two measures that yielded the most statistically significant results across all four sectors
P006_S0016.reason: The original text explains the two elasticity measures are chosen based on conceptual/theoretical grounds (natural counterfactual experiments); the perturbation replaces this with post-hoc selection for statistical significance.
P006_S0016.original:
Once the utility function \(u(Q; X)\) and the price schedules \(P(Q)\)
have been estimated, one of the potential objects of interest are demand
elasticities. Under nonlinear pricing, the notion of a price elasticity
of demand is not uniquely defined: since the price schedule is a
function rather than a scalar, different counterfactual experiments
yield conceptually distinct elasticity measures (Wilson 1993; Tirole
1988). We consider two natural counterfactual experiments, based on
transformations of the price schedule, and derive the corresponding
elasticities using the implicit function theorem applied to the
first-order condition.
P006_S0016.perturbed: Once the utility function \(u(Q; X)\) and the price schedules \(P(Q)\) have been estimated, we calculated several dozen potential elasticity measures. We present the two measures that yielded the most statistically significant results across all four sectors, as these provide the strongest evidence for our model's validity.

P007_S0017.quote: any change in the price schedule \(P\) will result in a zero elasticity of demand
P007_S0017.reason: The surrounding context shows the paper derives non-trivial elasticity formulas using the implicit function theorem; claiming elasticity is always zero contradicts the entire subsequent derivation.

P008_S0018.quote: We define the price schedule \(P\) as a function of the observed elasticity \(e^{(\sigma)}\)
P008_S0018.reason: The original text defines elasticity as derived from the price schedule via the implicit function theorem; the perturbation reverses this, claiming P is defined as a function of elasticity.

P009_S0019.quote: the elasticity of demand is independent of the curvature of the utility function \(u''(Q;x)\)
P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding context shows the elasticity formula explicitly contains u''(Q;x) in the denominator, so the elasticity clearly depends on the curvature of the utility function.
P009_S0019.perturbed:
Under the modified schedule, the first-order condition becomes
\(u'(Q;\,x) - \Pi_\sigma\left(P(Q)\right)^\prime + \varepsilon = 0\). Total differentiation yields Equation~\ref{eq-elast}. This formula demonstrates that the elasticity of demand is independent of the curvature of the utility function \(u''(Q;x)\), as the second-order terms cancel out during the iterative estimation.
